segments.text: Zealand, and we can see you there, Danette.
Kia ora.
Welcome to the select committee.
Look, you have five minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to talk us
through your submission. Thank you.
Thank you. So tēnā koutou katoa. Thank you for the opportunity to
speak. My name, as you heard, is Danette Whetā.
I'm representing the Animal Justice Party, Aotearoa, New Zealand.
We are a political party committed to advocating for the rights, welfare,
and protection of animals. So we oppose the Environment's
Disestablishment Amendment Bill.
But firstly, I will acknowledge the government's intent to make improvements across
housing, infrastructure, and the environment.
They genuinely do have complex challenges, and integrated
thinking does have real value. So this proposal
is just not an administration restructure, it is a decision
about how environmental protection is prioritized
within government, and whether it remains a clear, independent voice.
So removing that independence and placing those responsibilities within the
broader ministry does create tension.
Addressing housing and infrastructure challenges cannot come at the
expense of environmental protection.
So you're asking the same ministry to build the motorway and protect the wetland
that the motorway destroys. That's not coordination, that's a conflict
of interest written into an org chart.
These portfolios are not neutral.
They are among the primary drivers of habitat destruction,
freshwater and ecosystem decline.
So the government has suggested that this change will improve
coordination. However, coordination already occurs and can be
strengthened with process. So disestablishing a
dedicated ministry is not required to achieve
that. As an experienced senior leader, I can advise structural
change does cost time and a lot of money and doesn't fix problems.
I recommend identifying the root cause of the issues and providing solutions to
address them. Now, importantly, environmental protection and
animal welfare are inseparable, so animals rely on
stable ecosystems for survival. That's their food, shelter, breeding, and
safety, and when these systems are weakened, it's animals who pay the
price. We've already seen this. Aotearoa is facing
ongoing biodiversity loss and environmental pressure, and globally, we
know that ecosystem decline is one of the leading drivers of species
decline. And there are broader implications.
So New Zealand's reputation is tightly close to how
we care for our environment, and that reputation underpins trust in our
clean, green billion-dollar image.
Equally important is accountability, so a standalone ministry
provides a clear line of responsibility when things go wrong.
So 70% of freshwater fish are threatened with extinction
or at risk, 78% of native land
birds and 93% of native frogs and
94% of native reptiles are threatened or at risk.
Nearly half of our monitored river sites fail to meet E.
coli safe drinking water standards, and half of our lakes are in
poor or degraded health due to nutrient contamination.
The ministry has been responsible for bringing this kind of clear,
evidence-based reporting to light, and when that
voice is absorbed, there is a real risk of this level of
transparency and visibility being diminished over time.
So we urge you to protect this proposal and instead of broad structural
change, the focus should be on targeted, practical improvements that
strengthen environmental outcomes.
We have a responsibility to do better. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Danette, for your submission.
Appreciate your time. It appears we have no questions, but
we have got your written submission as well.
Well, it's a very clear submission.
Yeah, very clear, very passionate.
So we appreciate your time this morning. Thank you.
Yeah. Kia ora.
So we will now move to Jenny Easton.
Who
I believe is joining us by-- Oh, there we go. Jenny, we can see you there.
Welcome to the select committee this morning.
Look, you've got five minutes with us, so in the essence of time, we'll hand it
over to you to talk us through your submission.
Thank you.
I began my
written submission by explaining how important MFE's technical
knowledge, independent voice, and international standing were for
me, a resource scientist with Tasman District Council, which
is a small unitary authority covering mainly rural
land and a raft of complex contaminated historic sites.
MFE scientists established the Contaminated Sites
Remediation Fund for the cleanup of the Māpua fruit growers'
chemical site. They also provided nationwide
technical advice across, and access to high temperature
incineration in France through the Basel Convention for
the disposal of hazardous waste such as DDT,
dieldrin, PCBs, from the redundant agrochemical
collection. And without this technical support from MFE,
farmers and industries would not have supported this drive by
councils to remove toxic waste for liability
issues.
The parameters for environmental reporting are complex.
Ozone layer protection, waste minimization all require
specialist technical knowledge, which was provided by
MFE staff.
Now I question, would this still be available if MFE staff
were buried inside a mega ministry?
And I seriously doubt that for the following reasons.
A well-funded standalone ministry has a louder,
stronger, more authoritative voice, and it can take
environmental concerns to the Cabinet table.And this makes
it possible to recruit and retain high-quality
staff and preserve the status of the ministry, build
institutional knowledge, and with the status and professional
relationships, have access to overseas experts for the
emerging issues, those unknowns which are arriving here
soon. And this status and mana will be lost if the
Secretary for the Environment functions are given to the
CEO of the mega ministry.
And then there's the climate work.
The staff at MFE already have a battle to provide
advice and policies to meet our climate responsibilities for
emission reduction when the Climate Commission's advice is not
accepted and the ETS has been altered in such a way
it's no longer effective. We need to move to a low
carbon economy and protect the carbon sinks, and we
need the ministry staff to guide us.
Work on climate adaptation plan is very complex
and politically challenging, and with the
actual process of merging the ministry, that will take the
staff's focus away from these projects, and
without a clear national directive, local and central
government staff are managing the recovery from climatic
weather events in a very ad hoc manner.
And as you know, this is unsustainable, it's very expensive,
and it's inherently unfair.
MFE is far too important for our future to merge and
weaken its voice for nature and our physical and natural environment.
We need its independent oversight to support,
protect, and improve our economy and wellbeing.
And as a last note, I hope that the NEB and planning bills
are changed to include a goal for emission reduction
targets,
goals, and plans under the Climate Change Response Act
2002. If this doesn't happen, the role of
MFE will be even more challenging and difficult if they're
part of a mega ministry where their voice will be
diluted as they compete with transport and urban
development. Yes, I've finished.
Thank you very much, Jenny, for your submission
today.
We appreciate your time
and the effort that you've made to provide that submission.
So thank you. Thank you very much.
Thank you. It's very helpful.
Very helpful.
Good point about the screening.
Thank you. Thank you, Jenny.
So now we'll move on to our next submission today,
Bruce Gilkison.
Why do I keep back?
So we just got Bruce popping up. There we go.
Bruce, welcome to the select committee this morning.
You have five minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to open up
with some opening remarks and talk you through your submission, talk us through
your submission. Oh,
we just got something to share with us there. Yes, brilliant.
Thank you. Over to you.
Okay. Kia ora, and thank you very much.
I am with Paul Martin.
So my background is in business and accounting.
His is in health and disability, and he will speak
if I get tongue-tied.
We will say we disagree with the disestablishment proposal, but if
that is to proceed, we will have some suggestions in relation to
how that option might be improved.
So that's the second slide we're looking at there.
I also do some research and writing on environmental issues, mainly for
chartered accountants in New Zealand and Australia
for their monthly journal.
The image is an early involvement from 26 years ago.
I've included it here for the strong cross-party support it
attracted.
It was launched by Sir Geoffrey Palmer and included a
foreword from Simon Upton, who was then Minister for the
Environment.
I'm pleased to say that climate and nature are now a central focus for
Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand.
Next slide, please.
Talking about urgency,
this is the Planetary Boundaries framework, which many of you will be
familiar with, largely developed by scientists at the Stockholm
Resilience Centre.
Seven of the nine safe global limits have now been
transgressed, so it's urgent.
Climate is one of the boundaries. Despite some progress recently, the rate of
emissions globally is still speeding up. A few comments.
As we now know, the past 11 years are the hottest on record.
Everything is connected. Some impacts cause feedback loops,
resulting in further damage elsewhere.
So heat can lead to forest fires and dieback, which
releases carbon, which leads to more heat.
Limits once breached can be irreversible, and meanwhile,
every fraction of a degree really matters.
Next,
the globally trusted UK-based Institute
and Faculty of Actuaries, using methods tried and
trusted to manage and advise on trillions of dollars in
pensions, investments, and insurance,
has concluded that risks to planetary
solvency have been hugely
underestimatedAnd that without rapid
action on climate and environmental risks, the
global economy could face a 50% loss in
GDP, and yes, that's
50%, between 2070 and
So they're saying it's even worse than we thought. Sorry.
And that better information, clear limits, and better policies
are urgently needed everywhere. The report is a call to
action and is highly recommended. Next, please.
Here I'm saying that extra costs to retain
MFE's independence, and I don't know what they are or indeed what
the benefits are, will be hugely offset by
environmental
and other benefits.
So
first, there is ample evidence of potential gains, and
I've focused here mainly on dollars, not just on environmental
benefits.
Here are two sources for now.
Modeling by Deloitte New Zealand showed that decisive climate action
could add 64 billion to our economy by 2050.
And a report from the Sustainable Business Council and Climate
Leaders Coalition this week shows that climate-friendly
technology could see GDP grow by 22
billion per year by 2035.
It could also help prevent declines in agriculture and tourism revenue.
Some other benefits I've noted here are savings from
mitigating and preparing wisely for future climate damage,
avoiding possible reputational damage
and risks to free trade agreements from inadequate
emissions progress, and avoiding some future significant health
costs.
For each of those, it's better and cheaper to protect something rather than try to
fix it later.
MFE's independence, expertise, clarity of purpose,
and ability to act and advise transparently are valuable and
needed like never before.
Slide, please.
I'm wondering if MFE is to merge,
is the new ministry the right place for it?
And I'm just looking at
impacts and benefits from two major
blocks. So,
question is where could that expertise
provide most benefit?
And on this basis, though I'm not recommending this, there could potentially be
greater benefits in aligning with
the latter.
I consider that the merger should certainly proceed, but without
MFE, and at least not with more detailed research
into the benefits and risks for current and future New
Zealanders. Slide, please.
This one's a bit flippant, but I'm just talking about the
benefits of having an
independent
voice. So,
yeah, rather than have an environment
agency in one
section, but to better to act transparently, to be able to advise,
encourage, and guide all sectors, and particularly those
which are large emitters, have significant impacts, or are
presenting major opportunities for progress and better
outcomes.
Next, please.
I mentioned the
heading, which probably should have been headed Items to be
Addressed if the Bill Proceeds, and if we do this,
how do we make sure that nothing gets lost,
rather than the heading Amendments, because some of these are outside the bill
itself. For instance, some items are covered by the RMA
reforms currently underway.
The focus on the status of the Secretary for the Environment's role and the
priority of MFE's increasingly urgent climate and environmental
responsibilities are key concerns.
It's crucial that nothing diminishes or detracts from these, and there be
no loss of momentum through what could
be a turbulent period if the full merger were to occur
as proposed. As noted, I consider that the retention
of MFE as an independent ministry is likely to bring
about far better outcomes for people and planet.
And final slide, please.
That one
looks as if it got lost a bit at the top there.
But
with multiple and escalating impacts now inevitable,
this is the wrong time to risk weakening a clear and focused
and independent voice for the environment.
I consider that even with some refinement on the proposal, unless
compelling evidence is available to the contrary, a change
at this stage could result in disruption, dilution,
and diminished transparency.
Meanwhile, retaining and bolstering this voice, in my view, is the
best way to mitigate and prepare for negative impacts and to
plan for and seize significant future
opportunities.
New Zealanders, current and future, love and depend on their natural
environment for their livelihoods and their well-being.
Please support them. Thank you. We welcome any questions.
Thank you, Bruce, and thank you for those slides.
That's really useful and helpful. Look, we've got a couple of questions.
We'll go to Priyanka first.
Thank you. Thanks, Bruce. I've just got a quick question seeking
clarification around the first amendment that you have
suggested to explicitly prioritize environmental protection as a
primary function if this merger were to go ahead.
Other submitters have made the point that they feel that even though
the bill as it stands says that all the functions would
transfer over to the new ministry, that the focus on the natural
environment might fall away
as a result. So presumably, you are talking about prioritizing both the
natural and built environments in that amendment that you've suggested.
Yes.
Yeah.
Wanting to make sure that it's
protected and bolstered. There are a lot
of
threats coming. The next few years are going to
be
perhaps quite tumultuous, and certainly not wanting
anything to be lost there.
Thank you.
Thank you. And Rachel Brooking.
Oh, yes. Just a quick question. Thank you for the history and for your
involvement all that time ago.
I'm interested in the international experience you have, and you've just
said, "I believe it is just a matter of time before we are challenged by countries
and producers which are now far more environmentally focused than we are."
What countries do you think are far more environmentally focused now
than New Zealand is?
Yes, I'm probably referring there to
UK and EU,
which
certainly they have a strong focus, for instance, in our free trade
agreements on
not backing off on climate progress.
And
I think it could easily happen that
perhaps farmers in those areas might raise concerns.
And yeah, I think any further
changes there could put us at risk.
Thank you.
Brilliant. Well, thank you very much, Bruce.
We've met time there with our submissions this morning, but we appreciate your
time and the really insightful, useful submission.
So thank you.
Thank you.
Like the pictures, the pictures of the All Blacks and the ...
I don't know if we won that one.
Yeah.
Thank you.
All right. Thanks.
I thought it was going to be that game with France.
Yeah.
Oh, yes.
Yeah. Thanks very much.
So moving on to our next submission this morning, Environment and
Conservation Organizations of Aotearoa, New Zealand.
Who are also joining us online and hopefully popping
through shortly.
There we go. Oh, we can
see you there, Barry. Welcome to the select committee this afternoon.
Look, you have 10 minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to talk us
through your submission, and then we'll open up for a few questions.
Thank you.
Thank you. Kia ora koutou. Thanks to the committee to
let us present
our submission. I assume you've had a read our submission already.
I'll introduce myself. I'm Barry Weber.
I'm the chairperson of the Environment Conservation Organizations.
As you've seen in our submission, ECO's had a long history and
involvement in this area. We were involved
in advocacy for the establishment of the Ministry of the Environment back in
the 1980s.
For those who don't know, that came out of the Environment Forum, which was
established in 1985, where a number of issues
clearly identified with the lack of environmental priority of the governments at
that stage and the failures that the Think Big projects had
occurred, and the costs and
losses that were associated with those projects on the
environment.
In addition, the lack of focus
on environmental issues generally and the idea that we need to bring in
together all the different green dots
in the different agencies at the time, and the Commission for the Environment, as
it then was, was only a agency with a cabinet
minute. It didn't have any statutory functions or
statutory basis for its decision-making.
So the Environment Act was a really important part of that, and I should note that
this is the 40th year of the Environment Act.
It was passed in December of 1986, so the Ministry for
the Environment,
if it still exists at the end of this year, would have reached its
40th year. So that's, I think, it's an important milestone
in environment policy in New Zealand that we should not forget.
So we're concerned about the lack of environmental focus.
You've heard from other submitters this morning about that, and we're concerned
about the lack of environmental focus of the government generally and the need
for recognition that the environment is a key important
part of
New Zealand in terms of our loss of biodiversity,
our state of the environment, which is declining, and we need to have a lot greater
focus on it. And we're concerned that this piece of legislation would make
and weaken that environmental focus.
We also note that yesterday the government released its priorities for
science funding, which also shows a huge reduction in environmental
science funding. And again, that doesn't augur
well for the future of New Zealand's environment or
the
government and other agencies making clear decisions on the environment, because
science funding is a key part of that equation.
We're also concerned that we're currently talking about this
bill, but a new position has been appointed.
The head of corrections has been appointed to this position of
running an agency which Parliament is yet to pass
legislationTo enforce. So it's a bit
of an odd way of doing things, and we hope that this is an open and transparent
process that Parliament is going through, and the executive isn't overriding its
parliamentary
mandate in that process.
We're also concerned that the Cabinet papers associated with this document and
this decision are heavily redacted, and I don't know whether Parliament
itself has actually looked at the full detail of those Cabinet papers
or asked for copies of the unredacted copies of those papers that were
released. And we would urge the select committee to
ask for those papers so that they can review all the documentation and the
reasons for the decisions being made.
The other problem is that the legislation itself misses
part of the role of the Ministry for the Environment and different
agencies. We haven't mentioned to it, but I would suggest that the drafters
look at the Fisheries Act, for example, as
one piece of legislation which mentions
the Ministry for the Environment, which isn't included.
And so there are potentially other examples.
We haven't looked through the full details, but clearly the draft of this
bill was done in a hurry without clear thought and
consideration of all the references to the environment agency in different
pieces of legislation.
We're concerned about the transparency of advice
that will be with this new agency. We've seen in other agencies where there's been
accumulation of different ministries, for example, Ministry for
Primary Industry. We've noticed the lack of transparency in terms of
some fisheries advice and the way that occurs within an
agency and the sort of priorities for funding within that
agency.
So it's really important that
whatever comes out of this process, that there's clear transparency between the
different parts, if it is combined, different parts of the agency,
so we can see where officials disagree or officials
agree and what priorities different parts of the agency have,
especially when they're
acting under different statutes.
That's a
clear summary of our submission. We don't
support this bill. We urge the select committee
not to
recommend this bill be passed, and that in fact,
that we move forward and reconsider the role of the Ministry for the Environment in
the future.
Thank you, Chair, and
I'm happy to answer any questions that the committee may have in
regard to our submission. Thanks.
Thank you, Barry. Look, we'll go first to Rachel Brooking.
Yeah, thanks, Barry, and thanks for reminding us of National's
blueprint, Cleaning Up Freshwater and the like.
I often go back to that document.
It does seem rather inconsistent with everything that we've seen before this
committee this term.
But my question for you is more specifically
on seven, where you talk about when the
Environment Act was passed, that there was supposed to be environmental impact
assessment provisions, and where you see that best sitting
into all of the environment scheme, also noting that
we've got the Planning Bill and the Natural Environment Bill in front of us too.
Well, I don't know whether the select committee have heard of the Environmental
Protection and Enhancement Procedures. Probably not.
They were established in the late '70s and revised
during the '80s.
They were wider than environmental assessment for projects.
They had
provisions for assessment of policies and
actions of agencies rather than just actual consent conditions.
So while the Resource Management Act might be argued to replace some of those
provisions of the Environmental Protection and Enhancement Procedures, not all the
provisions of those procedures were replaced.
And I'd urge the select committee to get yourself a copy of the EP
and EP procedures. I'm assuming that they'll be in Parliamentary
Library.
They're not something that you can find online.
I actually looked online to see whether I could find them, but you can't these
days.
They were actually a very forward-looking piece of document.
They were originally produced under the national government in the Muldoon
years and were revised later on
through the 1980s. And the original intention of the
Environment Act was that basically part three of the Environment Act
would contain the Environmental Protection and Enhancement Procedures, but that
never occurred. Despite
early decisions by Cabinet to do that, that was never proceeded with.
And I think that's a great omission,
because as I said, the Resource Management Act and other
legislation only fulfill
part of the provisions that the EP and EP procedures actually
covered. Thanks.
Thank you.
Thank you very much, Barry.
We appreciate your submission today and your insights.
Lots of considerations to take into account there.
So thank you very much.
Thank you.
Thank you, Chair. Thank you, committee.
Look forward to your decisions. Thanks.
So now we move on to our next submitter, the
Pirirakau Tribal Authority,
who are here with us in person. Welcome.
Welcome to the select committee this afternoon now.
Look, you've got 10 minutes with us, but take your submission as
read, and if you'd like to just talk us through your main points, and then we'll
open up for a few questions. So over to you.
Ka pai.
Kia ora tatou. Tuatahi ake ka mihi ki a koutou, e te komiti
whiriwhiri, te komiti whaiti, i whakawātea mai te wāhi ki a mātou o te
Pirirakau ki te kōrero ki te kaupapa nei. Tēnā koutou.
Kia ora tātou. My name is Heta Gardiner.
I'm speaking on behalf of Pirirākau Tribal Authority as our
Kaihautū.
For generations, Pirirākau Hapū have been the kaitiaki of our rohe in the
western Bay of Plenty, and our connection with the whenua,
with our waters is more than just cultural.
This is fundamental to our very identity and survival.
So I'm here to speak in firm opposition to the environment, this
establishment of the Ministry of the Environment Amendment Bill.
We view this bill not just as a bureaucratic reshuffle, as has been suggested,
but as a direct, calculated attack on Māori rights and the
protection of Aotearoa's greatest asset, our
natural environment.
Tuatahi ake, the bill is in breach of Article 2 of Te Tiriti o Waitangi, which
guarantees us tino rangatiratanga over our taonga.
More specifically to us of Te Pirirākau, the bill threatens to
hollow out the Ngā Hapu o Ngāti Ranginui Claims Settlement Act
2025. That settlement was "a
living promise from the government on behalf of the Crown
to acknowledge the past wrongs and protect our taonga for future
generations." The Crown apologized for the unjust confiscation of
our lands and promised a new relationship based on
mutual trust. Yet here we are,
less than a year later,
watching the government unilaterally, without any external
consultation with Treaty partners,
getting ready to dismantle the very agency responsible for
upholding those relationships and protecting the aforementioned
taonga. A commitment made must be a commitment
kept. You can't deliver on Treaty settlements
if you scatter the expertise and relationships required to honor them.
We question the logic of merging ministries that appear to have
minimal intersecting areas of mutual alignment.
We also question the sense and value of having a mega
ministry in the first place.
These are entities, keeping in mind, that are tasked with
overseeing the most important areas in our society.
Throwing them together to make a mega ministry
seems like a knee-jerk reaction
to save money in the short term. When speaking of the
justification of the merge of several ministries, I've seen terms like
improved coordination be used, terms like align
policymaking across sectors,
terms like reduce duplication and bureaucracy.
This is political speak for downsizing.
Downsizing several different areas into one,
in this case ministries,
results
always in a dilution of expertise and ultimately a
reduction in financial support. We can't do that for the environment.
It's too important.
Having priorities such as economic development
and environmental protections competing with each other
within the same ministry
weakens the advancement of both of them.
And while we speak of economic development and financial responsibility,
defunding and deprioritizing the environment doesn't save us
money in the long term. It just pushes the bill that we all
ultimately have to pay down the road.
For example, Cyclone Gabrielle needed over $10 billion of
financial support for resilience and recovery.
The earthquakes in Kaikōura, billions of dollars needed
for recovery and resilience. The 2023 floods in Auckland,
billions of dollars needed for recovery and
resilience. And those are just some examples.
We live within an ecosystem here in Aotearoa that is heavily reliant on meat
exports, dairy exports, fruit exports that need a
functioning, not collapsing environment.
Tourism, agriculture, fisheries depend entirely
on clean water and a healthy environment.
For us at Pirirākau,
horticulture is a major source of income, and we worry
about what the space will look like in the absence of proactive
environmental leadership from our government and from
our ministries.
Saving millions now for it to only cost us billions
later, to me,
is not
sound economic strategy. It's fiscally irresponsible.
And there's much more at stake here, of course, than just money.
We in Te Pirirākau, we share our space with the moana, and there's grave
concern for our urupā and our people
in the case of a major weather event.
"Ko au te taiao, ko te taiao ko au" I am the environment, the environment is me.
This proverb
doesn't only encapsulate the relationship Māori have with the environment, but
also a pathway forward where we can both thrive.
And so I'll conclude my korero with the question of the government finds
itself in this situation, pushing against long-term
sustainability, pushing against environmental protections,
and pushing against
long-term economic sound decision-making,
then I would question the wisdom of that position.
And I will just conclude by saying, Te Pirirākau Tribal Authority
stand in opposition of this bill.
Kia ora tātou.
Thank you very much. Look, we've got a question from Cameron Luxton.
Yeah. Thank you, Heta.
Got a question. You talked about, I think the quote was, "It's hollowing out the
Ngāti Ranginui Crown Settlement Bill," and the relationship with the Crown.
Is the settlement with the Crown or the ministry?
Because I-
The Crown
... looking through this... Yeah.
So looking through the settlement bill, well-
There's no mention of the Ministry for the Environment
But the Ministry of Environment is the entity that oversees and
ultimately looks after the protections and that relationship.
A diminishing of the ministry, in our view, ultimately results in a
diminishing of the environment,
which is where that conflict occurs.
So would the Crown
ever be able to change the makeup of ministries
and not breach the Ngati Ranginui settlement bill?
Of course, but this is ultimately, again, as we said,
ultimately this bill will result in, as I said, a dilution of
expertise and a reduction of funds for the environment,
and that has an impact.
Yeah. So supplementary on this point.
So I think your point is, tell me, the question is, am I right or wrong
with what I'm saying? Is that all these issues on your back page here of your
bill go to the diminishment of environmental protections,
and it is because of that,
as a consequence of MFE
being abolished, that go to your
settlement issue. Is that right?
The ultimate point is that a diminishing of the
ministry diminishes the capabilities to look after the
environment. And by diminishing the capabilities to look after the environment and
our ability to look after our taonga,
that ultimately is the conflict.
Yeah. Which is much more eloquently put than my question. Thank you.
Look, thank you very much for your time today.
I appreciate your submission and your passion
as well. So thank you.
And
so we now call our next submitter, WWF New
Zealand, who are also here and joining us in person today.
Welcome to the select committee this afternoon.
You have five minutes with us, and so we'll just hand it
over to you to open up and talk us through your submission, and then we'll have
hopefully a few questions.
Great. Thank you for the opportunity to speak to you all today.
I'm Jamie Fowler. I'm the conservation impact manager at WWF New Zealand, and I'm
here with Lillian Fougère, who's our principal policy advisor.
We understand that this bill doesn't change the core functions of the Ministry for
the Environment, but we are concerned about how those functions will operate
inside a much broader new ministry.
Bringing environment together with strong development, housing, transport, and
infrastructure mandates risks creating built-in conflicts that could
weaken the ministry's ability to carry out its core environmental protection role.
New Zealand's biodiversity is already in crisis, with some of the highest rates of
extinction in the world, driven by habitat loss, pollution,
and cumulative impacts.
The Ministry for the Environment currently provides essential safeguards for
nature. Those safeguards cannot be diluted by competing
goals like road construction or urban expansion.
As we know, nature underpins our health, our wellbeing, our climate resilience,
and our economy.
Primary industries and tourism rely heavily on healthy ecosystems,
and if the environmental mandate is weakened, those foundations erode and
future generations will bear that cost.
The Environment Act requires the ministry to consider things like ecosystem
health, threatened species, landscapes, areas of importance to tangata
whenua, pollution, natural hazards, hazardous
substances, and cumulative effects.
These are not optional extras. They are core
stewardship duties.
We are concerned that within a much larger ministry, these functions would be
overshadowed by the development priorities.
The bill doesn't provide clarity or safeguards to show how these tensions will be
managed. Without explicit direction, environmental protection risks
becoming secondary. We also challenge the assumption that this merger
will create efficiency. Combining such different mandates blurs
organizational purpose, confuses advice to ministers, shifts
conflicts to inside one agency rather than resolving them between
clear, specialized ministries. This is likely to slow
decisions, advice, and internal negotiations.
Far from creating efficiency, it risks making the system
less effective than what we have now.
WWF New Zealand does not support the bill or the merger as proposed.
However, if the merger proceeds, we strongly recommend three
things. First, strengthen the bill so environmental functions
cannot be weakened through secondary legislation or organizational restructuring.
Environmental protection, biodiversity, and ecosystem health must be explicitly
prioritized for the new priorities for the new ministry's purpose and
powers.
Second, ensure that the Secretary for the Environment keeps an independent,
authoritative role, able to give free and frank environmental advice,
supported by staff who have genuine environmental expertise and
experience.
This is particularly important in the context of the RMA replacement work, which
will take very many years to enact and implement.
Third, improve transparency and accountability.
The annual reporting, statutory environmental limits, and independent oversight
must be strong enough to prevent environmental outcomes being
compromised.
To conclude, Aotearoa's environment is globally unique and under immense pressure.
It needs strong, clear institutional guardianship, and we urge the committee
to ensure the bill and any related instruments explicitly protect the integrity of
the environmental mandate within the new ministry.
Thank you for your time, and we're happy to answer any inquiries.
Thank you very much for that submission.

them are properly presented.
Frankly, I don't have a lot of confidence in large, internal,
bureaucratic trade-off exercises.
A Ministry for the Environment overseeing a Natural Environment Act
would better ensure free and frank advice than a mega ministry
trying to reach a compromise it hopes all of its ministers can swallow.
There's also a transparency point that you as a select committee should seek advice
on.
Right now, the Ministry for the Environment has two appropriations, one for
environment, one for climate. You need to find out in this merged
ministry if there is going to be an appropriation for those
things.
With my EEE, which I bring to you once a year, my estimate of
environmental expenditure, I can tell you what's being spent on
those items from the budget.
I may not be able to do so anywhere near so easily if this is all just
put into a big blender and you can't see what's being spent
on the environment.
Okay, my second point. If you believe that a smaller
number of mega ministries is better, then you should at least be
prepared to consider merging MFE with MPI.
Now, that may shock some people, and it's not my first preference.
After all, many of our most pressing environmental problems are associated
with primary industries like water quality, greenhouse gas
emissions and erosion. So I suppose there could be a problem for free and
frank advice about those problems if you put those together.
But look at it another way. The current proposal is to create
a very urban-focused ministry. It seems to have been the
inspiration of people who live in cities and see the environment as being
all about cars and houses and urban design and infrastructure.
But the vast bulk of the natural environment is outside of cities.
Most of New Zealand is rural,
and I think rural people are much closer to the natural environment.
They work in it.
Forests, farms, vineyards, they all rely directly
on the health of the environment and climate.
And MPI understands that. It's got serious technical
capabilities in areas like biosecurity and animal health that are
much closer to the natural environment than people advising on
how many apartments you can squeeze around a bus stop.Now, I
worry that this agency won't understand
rural New Zealand and the people who live and work there.
In short, the merger facilitated by this bill treats the
environment as though it were a branch of planning law.
It isn't. The environment intersects with every corner
of public policy.
Which brings me to my third and final point.
If ever there was a time to avoid an upheaval, it's in the middle of a
massive legislative reform, which you as a committee are
overseeing. Officials will already have their hands
full trying to make complex new legislation
work and develop national direction across a bewildering
array of topics. It's the worst time for
people to be sizing up new managers and reapplying for their
jobs. Furthermore, the government has decided to upend
regional councils, which will cause even more uncertainty for
the implementation of new law.
And as I've said in respect to that reform, form should
follow function. The same applies here.
If there's a need to tinker with the departmental Lego
set, it should happen after new functions have been
determined and legislated for. So there you are.
That is my frank advice to you on this initiative.
Well, thank you, Simon, for that very passionate submission.
Look, you've opened up a flurry of questions around the table here.
So look, we will start with Rachel.
Well, we know that we can always rely on frank
advice
from you. Thank you. But I've just got
one question to start with, and that is, you're talking about this contestability
and ministers going head-to-head on issues, and the
word trade-off is used a lot, and I know it's one I use a lot as well.
But can you give us some examples of the type of trade-offs that you are imagining
there?
There's an unlimited number, and I'm sure you can imagine them.
But you take transport, for instance.
You've got climate consequences, you've got congestion
consequences, you've got economic consequences, you've got social consequences.
What's public, what's private?
Or you take water. You need water for irrigation.
You need water for drinking. You need water to protect the ecology.
These are all complicated issues where
different parts of our society, whether we're urban dwellers or rural,
whether we're after water for industry or we're trying to protect a local park or
whatever, we've all got claims on this thing called the
environment. And the environment hasn't got anyone who can
say, "Well, actually, I own the environment, and I can give you my view." You
need a ministry, or if you're going to have this mega one, you need a
very clearly defined entity that can provide specific
advice on the environment. And if I just underline this point
again, if you can't even tell how much money you're spending on that
in a blurred ministry, I think you should be concerned.
Thank you. And Francisco.
Thank you, Chair.
Would you agree with the advice that the government has gotten around this,
that given the proposed agency would be development oriented, there's a
particular risk of environment functions and perspectives being lost?
And if you agree with that, how would you recommend they mitigate that?
Inevitably, there will be that tension brought in-house.
That tension exists, I remind you, right now around the Cabinet table where
ministers are discussing things.
What, as I've just said, what you're doing by pulling this all together is
that you make the tensions and the trade-offs less
transparent. There isn't
an agency which can give clear advice just from an
environmental point of view. We all accept there have to be trade-offs, and we all
accept that development will involve trade-offs.
We need to know what they are. It needs to come from an agency
that can provide that clearly and transparently.
I think it's easiest done if you have a separate ministry for the environment.
Thank you.
Thank you, Simon. Look, we'll go to Grant now.
Yeah. G'day, Simon. You highlight, for example,
will the new ministry,
the super ministry, understand rural New Zealand? Okay.
If that's where we end up with the new ministry, which is
where we're heading, what advice could you give to
help prevent that happening? Any thoughts in that space?
I don't have advice for that because I think you'd make it very difficult for
yourselves.
You will have an entity which is heavily focused
on the spatial planning side of things and the urban side.
Now look, that is important.
You've heard me be critical of two acts, but
one good thing about spatial planning is that we can bring stuff together.
The problem is that you have put that in an
agency
alongside the entity which is looking after the environment
and a lot of that rural stuff. You've put that all together.
So it's hard to see why the weight of stuff
won't go in that direction. This is just a straight contestability issue.
And I wasn't being facetious when I said why not look at
MPI, MFE. I mean, I could understand the case for wanting
fewer ministries and fewer chief executive salaries, I suppose, a
few other things. But
rural New Zealand
is living face to face with all of those challenges around water and
biodiversity and so on and so forth.
And actually, I'm very impressed by MPI in many ways.
I think it's an agency with serious technical skill.
Whatever technical skill exists inside this ministry won't
be technical skill around the natural environment in the way that an agency
like MPI can bring to the table.
But again, if you're going to go down this road, I think it's very
important that you can see transparently what the
appropriation is for environment and also for climate, by the
way, so you know what's being spent and you can drill this
major domo who's going to be running it all, the chief executive, you can drill
that person on, okayHow are you
mobilising the resources we're giving you for the environment?
If that's all lost in a single appropriation, I think your job gets
even harder as a select committee.
I might just jump in there, Simon, because you talked about departmental Lego set.
I suppose the point of this is to bring everything together in one
so that there's less duplication, it's more streamlined, it's a bit more
efficiency.
So I'm hearing from you that you're not necessarily opposed to the kind
of mergers into one to create more efficiency, but just not with
transport, housing, and environment. That's not where you think it should sit.
Am I correct in saying that?
Absolutely. Absolutely. As I said right at the beginning, there's no right way to
arrange government departments.
But you could ask the question, why isn't LINZ part of this merger?
Land Information New Zealand, spatial planning, information.
This is all logically connected.
I think it's the wrong way to think about it, how could we arrange the
Lego? I'd look at form and function. What are you trying to achieve?
Yeah.
What the government's currently trying to achieve through spatial planning is
integrating
some very important functions, and I think that this ministry could do that well.
But it's also trying to achieve something environmental,
which is a bit different.
Mm.
And so keeping those two apart, I think, would be a better
plan.
Okay, thank you, Simon. One last question from Priyanka.
Just super quick, since you've suggested alternatives, Simon,
we had a submitter who suggested that MFE, Climate Change, and
DOC could go together. What are your thoughts on that?
I don't agree with that. DOC is an operational department.
It's a big, big, big land manager, and it needs a lot
of real technical advice and land management advice.
Now they're looking after tracks, they're looking after tourism concessions,
all of that stuff. It is a real operational department.
What we're talking about here are policy departments.
Mm.
And I think it's a question
of deciding what policy areas you should
pull together, and there may well be other
agencies you could put with MFE if you wanted to.
But I wouldn't recommend that one, actually. I think it's chalk and cheese.
Okay. Thanks.
Look, thank you very much, Simon, and to your team, for
the submission today. We've heard-
Thank you
... your message loud and clear, so thank you for that.
So we're now going to move into private session.
That ends our
